title: Reflect shapes
description: Given a figure and a definition of a reflection, manually draw the image.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Plot the image of triangle $\triangle ABC$ under a reflection across the $y$-axis.**

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]
hints:
A reflection moves a point perpendicularly across a line, so that the [[☃ definition 1]] is the same distance from the line as the [[☃ definition 2]], but on the other side.



[[☃ image 1]]

In other words, the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector between any pair of corresponding points in the image and the pre-image.
Since the $y$-axis is *vertical*, the reflection will move each point of $\triangle ABC$ *horizontally* across the axis.

For example, point $A$ is $2$ units to the right of the $y$-axis, so $A'$ will be $2$ units to the left of the axis.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can reflect each of the other vertices in the same way.



[[☃ image 1]]

This is the image of $\triangle ABC$ under the reflection.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
**Plot the image of triangle $\triangle ABC$ under a reflection across the $y$-axis.**

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]
hints:
A reflection moves a point perpendicularly across a line, so that the [[☃ definition 1]] is the same distance from the line as the [[☃ definition 2]], but on the other side.


[[☃ image 1]]

In other words, the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector between any pair of corresponding points in the image and the pre-image.
Since the $y$-axis is *vertical*, the reflection will move each point of $\triangle ABC$ *horizontally* across the axis.

For example, point $A$ is $3$ units to the left of the $y$-axis, so $A'$ will be $3$ units to the right of the axis.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can reflect each of the other vertices in the same way.



[[☃ image 1]]

This is the image of $\triangle ABC$ under the reflection.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
**Plot the image of quadrilateral $ABCD$ under a reflection across the $y$-axis.**

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]
hints:
A reflection moves a point perpendicularly across a line, so that the [[☃ definition 3]] is the same distance from the line as the [[☃ definition 4]], but on the other side.



[[☃ image 1]]

In other words, the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector between any pair of corresponding points in the image and the pre-image.
Since the $y$-axis is *vertical*, the reflection will move each point of quadrilateral $ABCD$ *horizontally* across the axis.

For example, point $B$ is $3$ units to the right of the $y$-axis, so $B'$ will be $3$ units to the left of the axis.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can reflect each of the other vertices in the same way.



[[☃ image 1]]

This is the image of quadrilateral $ABCD$ under the reflection.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
**Plot the image of triangle $\triangle ABC$ under a reflection across the $x$-axis.**

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]
hints:
A reflection moves a point perpendicularly across a line, so that the [[☃ definition 1]] is the same distance from the line as the [[☃ definition 2]], but on the other side.



[[☃ image 1]]

In other words, the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector between any pair of corresponding points in the image and the pre-image.

Since the $x$-axis is *horizontal*, the reflection will move each point of $\triangle ABC$ *vertically* across the axis.

For example, point $B$ is $5$ units above the $x$-axis, so $B'$ will be $5$ units below the axis.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can reflect each of the other vertices in the same way.


[[☃ image 1]]

We note that points $A$ and $A'$ are in the same place because $A$ was on the line of reflection.

This is the image of $\triangle ABC$ under the reflection.



[[☃ image 1]]

In other words, the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector between any pair of corresponding points in the image and the pre-image.
Since the $x$-axis is *horizontal*, the reflection will move each point of quadrilateral $ABCD$ *vertically* across the axis.

For example, point $C$ is $5$ units below the $x$-axis, so $C'$ will be $5$ units above the axis.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can reflect each of the other vertices in the same way.



[[☃ image 1]]

This is the image of quadrilateral $ABCD$ under the reflection.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:

For example, point $A$ is $5$ units above line $\ell$, so $A'$ will be $5$ units below $\ell$.

This is the image of $\triangle ABC$ under the reflection.
